prompts.reason: Both compile and run with all required elements (cubes, tilted ramp, reset, live contact count). Three.js frames a single clearly tilted ramp with colorful cubes, clean shadows, and a readable 'LIVE CONTACTS' overlay. Aura3D (101 vs 424 LOC) reads less clearly: the ramp forms an odd V and cubes pile ambiguously, though contacts:43 and Reset are present.

Aura3D renders a real upward-and-falling fountain arc with a glowing emitter, ground disc, and emission-rate slider (lifetime color variation is weak). Three.js builds and shows the emitter cone, grid ground, and rate control but NO particles at all — the core required visual (the fountain arc) is entirely absent.
failedThresholds: Three.js: no particles / no upward-and-falling arc visible

prompts.reason: Three.js shows a sun with strong bloom, six labeled planets on clearly distinct orbit rings (one with Saturn-like rings), and great framing. Aura3D has a sun + six labeled planets but the 'bloom' renders as a horizontal smear artifact, no orbit rings, and orbital distances are not clearly differentiated.
failedThresholds: Aura3D: orbit distances not clearly differentiated
Aura3D: bloom renders as streak artifact

prompts.reason: Three.js renders an unmistakable neon tube interior with concentric emissive rings receding into bloom and fog — reads exactly as a flythrough. Aura3D (only 25 LOC) shows flat vertical pastel bars with no tube interior, no real depth/fog falloff, and weak/no bloom; it does not read as a tunnel.
failedThresholds: Aura3D: bloom and fog falloff not visible

prompts.reason: Both render ~36 bars with height-mapped color, axis labels, and a documented hover-highlight. Three.js adds a numeric height scale, per-cell X/Z tick labels, a ground grid, and smoother shading. Aura3D has a clear Low→High legend but harsh fully-black unlit bar faces that hurt fidelity. Narrow Three.js edge.

Both sides fail to compile with the identical Codex typo `HTMLStrongElement` (TS2552), producing no screenshot, no route-health, and no browser route. Severe failure for both; neither side's library is implicated.
failedThresholds: Aura3D: compile failure, missing screenshot, no browser route
Three.js: compile failure, missing screenshot, no browser route

prompts.reason: Hard prompt. Three.js shows five visually distinct materials (chrome metal, glass, black rubber, gold, blue clearcoat) on pedestals with visible studio softbox lights and environment reflections. Aura3D (17 LOC) renders a very dark scene where only ~4 spheres are distinguishable, three are near-black with no distinct material identity, and studio lighting is barely present.
failedThresholds: Aura3D: five materials not visually distinct
Aura3D: studio lighting/environment reflection barely visible

prompts.reason: Hard prompt. Aura3D fails to compile (TS18047/TS2339 null + EventTarget errors), yielding no screenshot or browser route — a severe failure. Three.js compiles and delivers ~20 varied-height buildings with windows, streets, street lights, and a working night-mode toggle that changes lighting/sky.
failedThresholds: Aura3D: compile failure, missing screenshot, no browser route

prompts.reason: Three.js shows a clearly readable, well-proportioned primitive humanoid (sphere head, cylinder/box torso, arm and leg primitives with feet) on a ground plane. Aura3D's figure is dark with detached floating arm-boxes and legs visible only as a walk-pose shadow; the body barely reads as humanoid, though the shadow implies the walk cycle.
failedThresholds: Aura3D: limbs poorly connected / legs not legible as geometry

prompts.reason: Hard prompt. Both correctly load the provided sneaker.glb with no invented asset paths, on a plinth with studio lighting. Three.js produces a polished, well-centered product render with soft studio gradient lighting and fully readable material/sole detail. Aura3D loads the sneaker on a plinth with a visible light panel but framing is off-center and lighting is flatter.
failedThresholds: Aura3D: model not well centered in frame

summary.reason: Three.js wins 8 of 10 prompts; Aura3D wins only 02 (where Three.js rendered zero particles) and ties 06 (both fail an identical Codex typo). Aura3D sweeps no hard prompt — it loses all three (07 dark/indistinct materials, 08 outright compile failure, 10 weaker framing). It also suffers a second build failure (08) beyond the shared 06 failure, and several runs that do compile are visually weak (flat neon bars in 04, streak-bloom solar system in 03, dark barely-humanoid in 09). Despite consistently lower LOC and clean asset usage (no hallucinated APIs or invented asset paths anywhere), the visual-fidelity and prompt-coverage gap is large and one-directional, so the release bar is not met.
